abstract:
This article examines the recent resurgence of the World Federation of Trade Unions (WFTU), particularly its growing influence within the Congress of South African Trade Unions (COSATU). The author challenges the notion that the WFTU serves as a legitimate, democratic alternative to the International Trade Union Confederation (ITUC), arguing instead that it remains a "hydroponic" relic of Stalinism—an organization artificially sustained by the ideological legacies of the Soviet era rather than organic, grassroots labor movements.

By tracing the history of the WFTU from its 1945 origins and its subordinate relationship to Soviet foreign policy, the paper highlights a fundamental continuity in its functioning. Despite recent efforts to rebrand the federation as a modern, "class-oriented" movement under Greek leadership, the author contends that its core remains rooted in the authoritarian traditions of State-controlled labor organizations. Through a critical analysis of recent speeches and policy shifts from figures such as George Mavrikos and Zwelinzima Vavi, the article illustrates how the WFTU exploits "Third Worldist" and anti-imperialist rhetoric to appeal to unions in the Global South that are physically and historically removed from the realities of Eastern European Stalinism.

Ultimately, the author argues that while the ITUC suffers from a lack of political narrative and bureaucratic inertia, the WFTU represents a sectarian dead end. The paper concludes that the path toward a genuine, transformative international labor movement lies not in these legacy structures, but in the autonomous self-organization and networking of workers currently operating outside traditional bureaucratic hierarchies.
